coredata.dc:title: A dual-mode fluorometric and colorimetric sensor based on N-doped carbon dots for selective and sensitive detection of nitrite in food samples
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Jie
Zhou, Xiaoxiang
Lin, Xiaojie
Xia, Yi
Zhao, Li
Huang, Ke
Xiong, Xiaoli
Yu, Huimin
coredata.dc:description: A highly sensitive and selective nitrite sensing platform based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs), by means of a headspace single drop microextraction process, was constructed for dual-mode fluorometric and visual colorimetric determination of nitrite. In this system, the two-channel selective sensing of nitrite was achieved at the same time, with color ranging from colorless to red and N-CDs fluorescence quenching gradually. Meanwhile, gaseous NO was generated from the redox chemical vapor generation process and further extracted into a single drop, which effectively improving the analytical sensitivity and alleviating the matrix interference. By fluorescence and colorimetry, the limits of detection were calculated to be 0.03 and 0.08 μg mL−1, respectively, while 0.8 μg mL−1 nitrite was discernable by the naked eye. This method was utilized to analysis of diversified real food samples (vegetables, milk and ham), and the results were proved to be nearly consistent with a standard method. Furthermore, the storage conditions of vegetable juice have also been explored.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrite sensing
N-CDs
HS-SDME
Fluorometric
Visual colorimetry
Food samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of dual-mode fluorometric and visual colorimetric determination of nitrite in food samples.
(a) and (b) TEM images of 50 nm (inset: the particle size distribution histogram). (c) and (d) HRTEM images. (e) Raman spectrum. (f) FTIR spectrum.

(a) Excitation-dependent emission spectra. (b) Emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths. (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, with insets illustrating the optical photos under sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (d) Calculated CIE coordinates from the PL spectra of N-CDs and N-CDs + NO- 2.
Possible principle of the nitrite sensor.

(a) FL spectra (inset: corresponding optical photos under 365 nm UV light). (b) UV–vis absorption spectra (inset: corresponding optical photos under sunlight). (c) Changes of UV–vis absorbances and fluorescence intensities with increasing of time.

Optimization of nitrite sensing conditions (∼25 °C, 2.0 μg mL−1 NaNO2): (a) volume of NaNO2; (b) concentration of NED; (c) type of acid (3 M); (d) concentration of H3PO4; (e) concentration of KI; (f) equilibrium time.

(a) Relative changing values of fluorescence intensity and (c) absorbance values in a certain gradient concentration of NaNO2. (b) and (d) Corresponding calibration curves. (e) Corresponding digital images of droplets (NaNO2: 0–6.0 μg/mL).
(a) Selectivity and interference of this method (NO- 2: 2.0 μg/mL; other ions: 200 μg/mL). (b) Corresponding digital images.

Concentration of nitrite in (a) boiled vegetable samples on room temperature for 3 days; (b) lettuce juice for 3 days on room temperature and 2–8 °C. (c) Concentration of spinach juice for 3 days on room temperature and 2–8 °C. (d) Concentration of lettuce leaf juice for 3 days on room temperature and 2–8 °C.

head.ce:title: A dual-mode fluorometric and colorimetric sensor based on N-doped carbon dots for selective and sensitive detection of nitrite in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of the Evaluation and Monitoring of Southwest Land Resources, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610068, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of the Evaluation and Monitoring of Southwest Land Resources, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of the Evaluation and Monitoring of Southwest Land Resources, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610068, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly sensitive and selective nitrite sensing platform based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs), by means of a headspace single drop microextraction process, was constructed for dual-mode fluorometric and visual colorimetric determination of nitrite. In this system, the two-channel selective sensing of nitrite was achieved at the same time, with color ranging from colorless to red and N-CDs fluorescence quenching gradually. Meanwhile, gaseous NO was generated from the redox chemical vapor generation process and further extracted into a single drop, which effectively improving the analytical sensitivity and alleviating the matrix interference. By fluorescence and colorimetry, the limits of detection were calculated to be 0.03 and 0.08 μg mL−1, respectively, while 0.8 μg mL−1 nitrite was discernable by the naked eye. This method was utilized to analysis of diversified real food samples (vegetables, milk and ham), and the results were proved to be nearly consistent with a standard method. Furthermore, the storage conditions of vegetable juice have also been explored.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite is widely found in the human environment and acts as a natural component of water and food (vegetables, fruits, meat, etc.) [1,2]. During the natural nitrogen cycle, reductase produced by microorganisms can convert nitrate into nitrite [3,4], meanwhile, nitrite in food may also derive from the artificial addition to perform the function of preservative and color protection [1,4–6]. It proves that excessive ingestion of nitrite was harmful to human body [2,7], and in presence of stomach acid, nitrite could react with some certain amines in food to generate strong carcinogens of N-nitrosamines [8–11]. The World Health Organization (WHO) and the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) have proposed a health standard of 432 mg/kg nitrate in edible parts of vegetables [3], while Chinese national standard of GB 2760–2014 explicitly stipulates that nitrite in meat products does not exceed 500 mg/kg. However, nitrite contents may reach more than 400 mg/kg in corrupted, pickled, or improperly stored vegetables [3].

The safety of nitrite in food has been generally concerned [4], and related detection methods are mainly consisted of ion chromatography (IC) [12,13], fluorescence spectrometry [14], spectrophotometry [15], electrochemical analysis [16] and chemiluminescence (CL) [17]. These relatively large laboratory instruments, usually with characteristics of high cost, cumbersome operation and long analysis time, are not conducive to field analysis [8,18]. Up to date, visual colorimetric method has been widely utilized to detect nitrite in the field, mainly due to their superiority in quick naked eye observation [15]. The common colorimetric strategies are based on the diazotization between nitrite and azo dye or the oxidation process of nitrite in acidic condition [15,19]. Chen et al. developed a highly selective chromogenic probe by the reaction of nitrite and amino group in 3, 3’, 5, 5’-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to form an orange diazonium salt [20]. Hong et al. utilized the redox reaction between nitrite and iodine ions to indirectly analyze nitrite in food [21]. However, the colorimetric problems in stability and sensitivity should not be overlooked, and thus a dual-mode fluorometric and colorimetric method have emerged which integrating the merits of high sensitivity and stability, and being conducive to field analysis [14,18]. Fluorescent manganese doped carbon dots (Mn-CDs), amino modified MIL-101 coated by polyvinylidene fluoride/polyvinylpyrrolidone (NH2-MIL-101@PVDF/PVP), Fe-single-atom catalysts (Fe SACs), poly-tannic acid nanoparticles (FPTA NPs), silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs) and so on, combining with the diazotization, nitrification process, inner filter effect or redox, have been reported to perform colorimetric and fluorometric detection of nitrite [15,22–24]. In these fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CDs) are superior with their simple synthesis, low cost, good solubility, low toxicity and excellent fluorescence properties [25,26].

Current fluorometric or colorimetric methods were mostly based on aqueous solution reaction in the form of analyte ions, which inevitably involving the sample matrix interference. To solve the problem, the headspace single drop microextraction (HS-SDME) technique has been effectively utilized [27,28]. In HS process, analyte was converted to gaseous substance by the sample introduction technique of chemical vapor generation, greatly alleviating the interference caused by sample matrix [28–30]. At the same time, gaseous substance was extracted into the droplets to further improve the sensitivity. Tang et al. used 1.5 mL capped polypropylene centrifuge tube as the reaction vessel, in which silver-gold core-shell nanoprism (Ag@Au-np) colorimetric reagent was fixed on the bottom of tube lid by surface tension and intermolecular force, thus to effectively avoid the potential matrix interference and achieve sensitive detection of H2S with LOD of 65 nM [31]. The improved devices have also been applied to generate gaseous AsH3, SO2 and iodine vapor that were further headspace micro-extracted by droplets to achieve colorimetric reaction [30,32,33].

In this paper, a dual-mode fluorometric and visual colorimetric sensing platform, based on amino-functional N-CDs, and assisted by HS-SDME technique, was constructed for selective determination of nitrite. N-CDs synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal assay, with 1, 3-diaminoguanidine monohydrochloride as nitrogen source, integrated the merits of strong fluorescence at 450 nm, low toxicity, simple synthesis and good water solubility. N-CDs was diazotized by NOx and then coupled with naphthalene ethylenediamine hydrochloride to form a red azo dye, resulting in color enhancement and fluorescence attenuation, prior to color discrimination by the naked eye and analysis by a fluorescence spectrophotometer, simultaneously. In detection of nitrite, analyte was reduced to gaseous NO and then absorbed into the single drop, greatly alleviating the matrix interference. The developed miniaturized sensing platform was potential in sensitive, selective and on-site analysis of nitrite in food.

All reagents were at least analytical grade, and solutions were prepared with 18.25 MΩ·cm deionized water (DIW) produced by the purification system of Chengdu Ultrapure Technology Co. Ltd. (Chengdu, China). 1, 3-Diaminoguanidine monohydrochloride (DGM) and N-(1-Naphthyl) ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (NED) were both purchased from Aladdin reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China), while citric acid (CA), KI, H3PO4, HCOOH, CH3COOH, H2SO4, HCl, HNO3 and other reagents (Fe3+, Al3+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Na+, NH+ 4, K+, PO3- 4, SO2- 4, SO2- 3, Cl-, NO- 3 and HCO- 3) were all from Kelong reagent Co. Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Real food samples, including ham, milk, milk powder and vegetables, were collected from local supermarkets (Chengdu, China). The water samples were taken from Chengdu city in China with locations of Jinjiang river, Dongfeng river, Qinglong lake and Sichuan Normal University (drinking water, tap water and pool water).

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra were collected by a Yoke T2602 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China) and a Gangdong F-320 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tianjin, China), respectively. Hydrothermal reactors (80 mL) from Suzhou Shenghua Instrument Technology (Suzhou, China) were used for synthesis of N-CDs，and the structure was characterized by Bruker Optics VERTEX 70 fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Germany) and Renishaw inVia Raman spectroscopy (Germany), while the lattice and morphology of N-CDs were acquired by JOEL JEM-2100 Plus transmission electron microscopy (Japan). The homemade HS-SDME device in this experiment is mainly composed of a 20 mL headspace reaction bottle and a 2.5 mL syringe, which are used for holding the sample solution and injecting the reductants (potassium iodide in phosphoric acid), respectively. Significantly, the container for microextraction, a 2 mL centrifuge tube lid, is fixed onto the inner cap of headspace bottle to hold the droplet of absorbent (N-CDs and NED) by surface tension.

N-CDs was prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal assay. Specifically, DGM and CA in a molar ratio of 2:1 were placed in a teflon high-pressure reactor and then heated in an oven at 150°C with 2 hours. After the reaction was completed, the solution changed from colorless to brownish yellow, preliminary indicating the formation of N-CDs. The product was then purified in a dialysis bag, prior to low temperature storage and use. N-CDs was also freeze-dried for further characterization tests [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of nitrite by this method
ce:section.ce:para: In the chemical vapor generation process, NOx was produced by adding a mixture of potassium iodide and phosphoric acid to the analyte nitrite. Firstly, the nitrite was accurately weighed, dissolved, and prepared to be 1.0 mg/mL stock solution with deoxygenated deionized water, which was stored under low-temperature conditions. Then the stock solution was diluted to a required concentration and placed in a 20 mL headspace bottle at room temperature, followed by the addition of 12 mM KI and 2 M H3PO4 by a syringe. NOx was immediately generated and then absorbed into the droplets that was hung on the inner cap of headspace bottle. In the self-made HS-SDME device, twice diluted N-CDs and an equal volume of 40 μg/mL NED were mixed, and hung in droplets. After reacting with NOx for 50 min, the droplets were taken out for colorimetric and fluorescence analysis, and the color changes would also be recognized directly by the naked eye. In the experiment, a high temperature would cause the generation of a great amount of steam to be sucked into the single drop, thus affecting the chromogenic process and further leading to the detection deviation. Therefore, to simplify the experimental operations and further apply to field analysis, the whole experiment was carried out at room temperature (∼25 °C). Besides, in the sensing system, the relative humidity was nearly 100 %.

In sample handling process, referring to GB 5009. 33–2016, ham sausage and milk powder high in protein were accurately weighed with 20.0000 g and dissolved in 90 mL deionized water, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Additionally, 10 mL 220 g/L zinc acetate was added to precipitate the protein. Thirty minutes later, centrifuge at 10000 rpm and collect the supernate for use. For high protein milk, similar operations were performed with 90 mL liquid sample. In contrast, vegetable samples were treated to obtain vegetable soup or juice, prior to filtration and use. The treated sample solutions were detected by this method and related results were further verified by a standard Griess method (GB 5009. 33–2016). In particular, nitrite was diazotized with 4 g/L 4-aminobenzene sulfonic acid in 20 % HCl to form diazo salt, and then reacted with 4 g/L NED to form a purple red dye at room temperature prior to UV–vis detection.

The morphology and lattice of the synthesized materials were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in
Fig. 2. It shown a good dispersion and nearly spherical shape with particle size of about 3.0 nm, which were consistent with related references of CDs [1,14]. A good lattice fringe (0.32 nm relatively loose lattice spacing) was verified by high resolution TEM (HRTEM), as shown in Fig. 2d. Raman spectroscopy further demonstrated the crystallographic information (Fig. 2e.), which shown a disordered D band (1329 cm−1) and crystalline G band (1570 cm−1). D band represents the defect of carbon material, while the ID/IG value of 1.34 was caused by the sp2 hybrid C-C vibration of carbon material, indicating that the insertion of N atom into carbon backbone leaded to a certain disordered structure [26]. Additionally, the surface functional groups of carbon material were characterized by FTIR (Fig. 2 f.). Absorption bands at 3650 and 3106 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of O–H or N–H, and characteristic absorption bands of CO and CN appeared at 1724 and 1683 cm−1, respectively. The peak at 1190 cm−1 corresponded to the C-O stretching vibration of alcohol functional group, while 1410 cm−1, 1071 cm−1 and 792 cm−1 were ascribed to C–N, C-O-C and C-O-H, respectively [35,36].

The spectral properties of N-CDs were comprehensively tested. By scanning different excitation wavelengths, related emission spectra were shown in Figs. 3a and 3b., and the optimally and characteristic absorption wavelength are located at 365 nm (red line) in Fig. 3c. Upon excitation with 365 nm radiation, the best emission wavelength of N-CDs was 450 nm. CIE can not only reflect the color of material but also the spectral characteristics. After addition of NO- 2, CIE coordinates moved from (0.15, 0.24) to (0.14, 0.28) as shown in Fig. 3d., accordingly, the blue fluorescence significantly weakened. Therefore, by CIE coordinate pairs, it was further proved that the obtained N-CDs had great potential in fluorescent visual sensing [14].

In the HS-SDME device, analyte of nitrite could react with H3PO4 and KI to generate NO by chemical vapor generation process, as shown in Fig. 1, and then NO was rapidly oxidized to NO2 in the air, followed by the conversion to N2O4 at room temperature. Meanwhile, instability N2O3 was produced by reaction between NO2 and NO, which was easily decomposed to NO and NO2 again [37]. In this system, NO2 was the major component among NOx [38]. The electrophilicity of NOx was much stronger than nitrite ion, hence it was easier to form NO under acidic condition. Specifically, as shown in Fig. 4, electrophilic reaction reacted on the surface of N-CDs, and amino groups was converted to be an intermediate, prior to the generation of diazo salt through proton transfer dehydration[34,39]. The rich amino groups of N-CDs were derived from the raw material of DGM. Subsequently, the generated diazo salt was coupled with NED to form azo dye, and the color of droplets changed from colorless to red, along with the decrease of fluorescence intensity [5]. In this way, a dual-mode fluorometric and visual colorimetric sensing platform was constructed for determination of nitrite with details shown in Fig. 4.

To verify the feasibility of experimental scheme, the corresponding FL spectra, UV–vis absorption spectra and optical photos of analytes and control groups have been explored. As shown in Fig. 5a, N-CDs alone had strong fluorescence emission at 450 nm, while the fluorescence of NED was relatively weak, and there was no significant change in both of them after addition of nitrite. Noteworthily, when nitrite was added, the fluorescence intensity of mixture (N-CDs + NED) decreased significantly. Correspondingly, it could be seen from the absorption spectra in Fig. 5b that an obvious visible absorption appeared at 540 nm in presence of N-CDs + NED + NO- 2, accompanied by colors changing from colorless to red, while no significant changes happened in other control assays. Thus, a dual-mode fluorometric and visual colorimetric sensing of nitrite was achieved. With the increase of nitrite content, the droplets color could gradually redden, along with a weaker fluorescence. Furthermore, a long-term stability test was carried out, and it shows that the absorbance of reaction system gradually reduced after 25 min, while the fluorescence intensity was relatively stable in a long time of 60 min (Fig. 5c). A good stability of this method, especially by the fluorescence detection mode, was conducive to the practical application and field analysis.

To get better performance, related experimental conditions have been comprehensively investigated, mainly including conditions of N-CDs (synthetic ratio of raw material and N-CDs dilution times) and nitrite sensing (volume of NaNO2, concentration of NED, type of acid, concentration of H3PO4 and KI, and equilibrium time of nitrite sensing). To eliminate the interference from external factors, the relative fluorescence intensity of (F0-F)/F0 was used to judge the fluorescence change.

Firstly, in order to successfully synthesize N-CDs with excellent fluorescence performance, by using hydrothermal synthesis method, a mixed solution of DGM and CA with different molar ratios was experimented, and it was found that the optimal molar ratio was 2:1 judged by fluorescence intensity (Fig. S1a). Meanwhile, due to the self-absorption phenomena, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs solution with high concentration was reduced, thus it was necessary to carry out an appropriate dilution. As Fig. S1b, when the volume ratio of N-CDs to water was 1:3, the fluorescence intensity was strongest. Thus, synthetic ratio of raw material (2:1) and N-CDs dilution times (VN-CDs:
V
H
2
O
= 1:3) were chosen in subsequent experiments.

The optimum conditions of nitrite sensing were also explored by this method, as Fig. 6. The optimal reaction volume of sodium nitrite was 5 mL, as shown in Fig. 6a. As a coupling object, NED was the key substance in visual colorimetric sensing, and the best concentration of NED was proved to be 40 mg/L (Fig. 6b). Effect of acid types used in chemical vapor generation process have also been investigated, common weak acids and strong acids were explored as shown in Fig. 6c, in which HCl and HNO3 were highly volatile to possibly affect the sensing droplets, and 2 M H3PO4 did the best (Fig. 6d). On this basis, KI as the reductant was tested, with optimal concentration of 12 mM (Fig. 6e). Under the above conditions, the equilibrium time of sensing system was confirmed to be 60 min (Fig. 6f).

Under the optimal conditions, the dual-mode signal responses along with concentrations of nitrite were investigated, as shown in Fig. 7. At room temperature, with the increase of nitrite content, the color of droplets gradually turned red, accompanied by an incremental visible absorption at 540 nm, while the relative changing values of fluorescence intensity at 450 nm was reduced. For fluorescence detection mode, in the range of 0.2–3.0 μg/mL, a good linear relationship (y = 0.2437x + 0.0849) was achieved with linear correlation coefficient of 0.9905, and the detection limit of nitrite was calculated to be 0.03 μg/mL. In analysis mode of UV–vis absorption spectrometry and visual colorimetry, as Fig. 7d, a good linearity (R = 0.9984) ranged from 0.8 to 6.0 μg/mL, with detection limit of 0.08 μg/mL. The corresponding colorimetric pictures are displayed in Fig. 7e, and the discriminable minimum concentration to the naked eye is proved to be 1.0 μg/mL. Stability tests of the reaction system were conducted by using 2 μg/mL nitrite. The relative standard deviation of fluorescence analysis was calculated to be 1.27 % by seven consecutive tests, and the corresponding colorimetric images, distinguished by the naked eye, were nearly similar in red color (Fig. S2a), proving a good stability of this method. In addition, the stability of synthetic N-CDs was investigated during 50 days. Under the same detection conditions, the fluorescence intensities were almost consistent (Fig. S2b).

By using this method, electivity and interference from common ions have been fully studied, and related information of interferents was described in Fig. 8. It both shows a good selectivity for nitrite, no matter in fluorescent or visual colorimetric sensing mode. When alone coexisting with these ions (NO- 2: 2.0 μg/mL; interferents: 200 μg/mL), recoveries of nitrite ranged from 102 % to 111 %, which verifying the excellent anti-interference ability of HS-SDME based method, and further displaying the great potential in analysis of real samples. In addition, the special selectivity tests were carried out aiming at common gaseous interferents, and it proved that the interference was ignorable.

Comparing with other single-mode sensing methods, fluorometry or colorimetry, this dual-mode sensor integrated the merits of both methods in sensitivity, simplicity and operability. In addition, the one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of N-CDs was relatively simple and the obtained products were stable. What's more, by HS-SDME, the matrix interference from real samples could be greatly alleviated, simultaneously improving the detection sensitivity. As listed in Table S1, LODs of this work are superior or comparative compared with other related sensing methods, and it shows the potential in field visual colorimetric analysis.

The proposed method was utilized to analyze common nitrite-rich food samples (hams, vegetables and milk) and varied water samples. The detection results were nearly consistent with date obtained from Griess standard method, and the spiked recovery rates were satisfactory (Table S2 and S3), which proving the reliability and practicability of this method. Nitrite contents in measured samples could meet the standard set by the European Union.

On this basis, the effect of vegetable storage methods and time on nitrite contents were comprehensively studied. Firstly, the simplified sample treatment method for vegetables in this work was successfully verified by GB 5009. 33–2016 national standard method (Fig. S4), which could effectively omit the cumbersome processing steps and improve the efficiency. By this way, boiled vegetable samples (lettuce, spinach and lettuce leaf) were treated and then analyzed, in which nitrite contents did not exceed the national limit. In the course of experiments, it was found that concentration of nitrite in vegetables would change with preservation time and storage means. Since cooked vegetable or vegetable juice cannot be stored for a long time, up to three days were chosen to carry out the experiment. Specifically, for all boiled vegetables, nitrite was not detected both on the first day at room temperature and within three days in 2–8℃ refrigerator. However, when stored at room temperature, a large amount of nitrite was found on the second day, which maybe owing to the reduction of nitrate to nitrite by microorganisms in vegetables. On the third day, content of nitrate was reduced, which was speculated to be a long-term exposure of nitrite to air to generate the oxidation product of nitrate [40]. These similar phenomena also appeared in different kinds of vegetable juice as Fig. 9b-d. Therefore, vegetable-related products were certainly suggested to store at low temperature, which could effectively reduce the generation of nitrite. Whether stored at room temperature or in refrigerator more than one day, the content of nitrite in vegetables juice increased greatly, thus try to eat less leftover vegetables.

In summary, this work constructed a dual-mode fluorometric and visual colorimetric sensing platform for highly selective, sensitive and matrix interference resistant detection of nitrite. In which, the obtained N-CDs as a fluorescence indicator was easily synthesized, low toxic and hyperfluorescent, and rich in amino groups to selectively sense NOx. By using a self-made headspace single drop microextraction device, the matrix interference was greatly alleviated, along with the improvement of detection sensitivity. On this basis, the dual-mode sensor not only achieved the highly sensitive and accurate analysis by large instrument, but also shown the potential in field analysis with color recognized by the naked eye. This method has been successfully utilized to analysis of diversified food samples. Especially, for vegetables, it proved that storing at room temperature could effectively reduce the generation of nitrite, and overnight storing would produce a great increase of nitrite.

Huimin Yu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Jie Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Li Zhao: Validation. Ke Huang: Methodology. Xiaoli Xiong: Supervision, Conceptualization. Xiaojie Lin: Validation, Methodology. Yi Xia: Validation. Xiaoxiang Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge Sichuan Provincial Youth Science and Technology Foundation (2024NSFSC1132) and the Foundation of Sichuan Normal University (No. ZZYQ2022003 and SYJS2022012) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: The manuscript is written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136045.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A colorimetric and fluorescent dual-readout probe based on red emission carbon dots for nitrite detection in meat products

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Yu, H. Zhang, Y.N. Liu, Y.L. Zhang, M.H. Shang, L. Wang, Y.T. Zhuang, X. Lv. A colorimetric and fluorescent dual-readout probe based on red emission carbon dots for nitrite detection in meat products. Food Chem. 374 (2021) 131768.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A large prospective study of meat consumption and colorectal cancer risk: an investigation of potential mechanisms underlying this association

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.J. Cross, L.M. Ferrucci, A. Risch, B.I. Graubard, M.H. Ward, Y. Park, A.R. Hollenbeck, A. Schatzkin, R. Sinha. A large prospective study of meat consumption and colorectal cancer risk: an investigation of potential mechanisms underlying this association. Cancer Res. 70 (2010) 2406–2414.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Plasmonic nanoparticles for colorimetric detection of nitrite and nitrate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Sepahvand, F. Ghasemi, H.M. Seyed Hosseini. Plasmonic nanoparticles for colorimetric detection of nitrite and nitrate. Food Chem. Toxicol. 149 (2021) 112025.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Methods for the detection and determination of nitrite and nitrate: A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q.H. Wang, L.J. Yu, Y. Liu, L. Lin, R.G. Lu, J.P. Zhu, L. He, Z.L. Lu. Methods for the detection and determination of nitrite and nitrate: A review. Talanta. 165 (2017) 709–720.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thread-based platform for nitrite detection based on a modified Griess assay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Singhaphan, F. Unob. Thread-based platform for nitrite detection based on a modified Griess assay. Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 327 (2021) 128938.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots for fluorescent detection of nitrite: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.M. Guo, R.Q. Wang, C.W. Wei, Y.J. Li, T.C. Fang, T. Tao. Carbon quantum dots for fluorescent detection of nitrite: A review. Food Chem. 415 (2023) 135749.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Assessment of potential nitrite safety risk of leafy vegetables after domestic cooking

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.H. Wu, Y.H. Liu, X. Cui, Q. Zhang, Y.P. Wang, L.P. Cao, X. Luo, J.H. Xiong, R. Ruan. Assessment of potential nitrite safety risk of leafy vegetables after domestic cooking. Foods. 10 (2021) 2953.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Huimin Yu received his BSc degree in 2013 and Ph.D degree in analytical chemistry in 2018 both from College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan. Since 2018, Huimin Yu has been working and researching in College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, Sichuan. In 2023, Huimin Yu was granted as associate professor.
